label: ai
topic: China's participation as the world's largest emitter
text:
China arrived in Paris carrying the memory of Copenhagen, where its negotiators were widely blamed for blocking a binding deal in closed-door sessions that other delegations were not invited to join. The reputational damage was considerable, and in the years between Copenhagen and Paris, Chinese climate diplomacy shifted noticeably. The joint announcement with the United States in November 2014 was the most visible signal: China committed to peak its carbon dioxide emissions around 2030 and to raise the share of non-fossil fuels in its primary energy consumption to around twenty percent by the same date. That declaration, and a follow-up statement in September 2015, helped break the logjam between the world's two largest emitters and gave the Paris talks momentum that had been absent from previous rounds.

The shift was driven by overlapping domestic and strategic interests. Chinese cities were suffering severe air pollution crises that generated widespread public anger and imposed heavy costs on the health system. The central government saw investment in renewable energy and electric vehicles not only as an environmental necessity but as an industrial strategy: by dominating the manufacture of solar panels, wind turbines, and batteries, China could position itself at the centre of the global energy transition rather than be constrained by it. By 2015, China was already the world's largest investor in renewable energy, spending more than the United States and Europe combined.

At the conference itself, China aligned with the European Union on several issues where it had previously sided with the Group of Seventy-Seven bloc. Most notably, China accepted the five-year ratchet mechanism, which it had initially opposed on the grounds that it would subject developing countries to an open-ended escalation process. Chinese negotiators also dropped their longstanding insistence that differentiation between developed and developing nations must be absolute and permanent, allowing the transparency framework to apply in modified form to all parties. This was a significant concession: previous Chinese positions had treated any obligation that applied equally to developed and developing countries as a violation of the principle of common but differentiated responsibilities.

The result was an agreement that, while still maintaining some elements of differentiation, moved substantially toward the universal framework that the European Union and the United States had sought. Chinese diplomats described this as pragmatism rather than capitulation, arguing that a functioning universal agreement served China's interests better than a rigid developed-developing divide that left the global climate problem unsolved. The shift was genuine but also partial: China continued to resist binding financial obligations and insisted that the agreement preserve the developing-country status that shields it from donor expectations, even as its economy had grown to become the second largest in the world.
